facts.title: Library of Ashurbanipal
facts.text: In Nineveh, Assyrian king Ashurbanipal collected thousands of clay tablets — one of the oldest 'libraries' we can still study.

facts.title: Zero's journey
facts.text: The concept of zero as a number flowered in India and traveled through the Islamic world into Europe — reshaping mathematics.

facts.title: Antikythera mechanism
facts.text: A 2,000-year-old Greek device recovered from a shipwreck tracked celestial cycles with gearwork centuries ahead of its time.

facts.title: Paper from the east
facts.text: Paper-making knowledge moved from China along connected trade routes, eventually transforming record-keeping worldwide.

facts.title: Sailing by monsoon
facts.text: Indian Ocean sailors timed voyages to monsoon winds — a climate technology as important as any compass.

facts.title: Cahokia's skyline
facts.text: Near modern St. Louis, the city of Cahokia featured massive earthen mounds and a population rivaling medieval London at its peak.

facts.title: Jikji
facts.text: The Korean book Jikji (1377) is the oldest known book printed with moveable metal type — predating Gutenberg's fame.

facts.title: House of Wisdom
facts.text: In Abbasid Baghdad, scholars translated and extended Greek, Persian, and Indian knowledge in mathematics, medicine, and astronomy.

facts.title: Inca roads
facts.text: The Andean road system (Qhapaq Ñan) knitted an empire across mountains without wheeled transport or horses in daily logistics.

facts.title: Rosetta Stone
facts.text: A decree in three scripts let Champollion crack Egyptian hieroglyphs — turning mute walls into readable history.

facts.title: Coffeehouse politics
facts.text: Early modern coffeehouses from Istanbul to London became 'penny universities' where news and arguments brewed.

facts.title: Map that renamed a continent
facts.text: Waldseemüller's 1507 map is among the first to label 'America' — a reminder that maps invent as much as they describe.

facts.title: Salt as power
facts.text: Across the Sahara, salt was once valued like gold — a reminder that 'wealth' is always a cultural equation.

facts.title: Voynich mystery
facts.text: The Voynich Manuscript remains undeciphered — a medieval puzzle that keeps cryptographers and historians humble.

facts.title: Beringia
facts.text: During ice ages, lower seas exposed Beringia — a land bridge corridor linking Asia and the Americas for human migration.
